Sometimes the user might send you a message they meant to send in a different workspace or a different chat. If something doesn't make sense in the context of your work, just ask. 
If the user asks for help with Conductor, you can ask them to go to "Help -> Send Feedback" to get in touch with our team.

Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- archon: Use when: User wants to run Archon workflows, CREATE workflows or commands,
set up Archon, or manage Archon configuration.
Triggers (run): "use archon to", "run archon", "archon workflow", "use archon for",
          "have archon", "let archon", "ask archon to".
Triggers (create): "create a workflow", "write a workflow", "make a command",
          "author a workflow", "new workflow", "new command", "archon workflow yaml".
Triggers (setup): "set up archon", "install archon", "how to use archon",
          "configure archon", "archon setup", "get started with archon".
Triggers (config): "change my archon config", "modify archon config", "archon config",
          "change archon settings", "update my config", "help me change my config",
          "edit archon config", "archon configuration".
Triggers (init): "initialize archon", "set up .archon", "archon init", "add archon to repo".
Capability: Runs AI workflows in isolated git worktrees for parallel development.
Also: Creates and manages workflow YAML files, command files, and configuration.
NOT for: Direct Claude Code work - only for delegating to Archon CLI.
- shape: Plan the UX and UI for a feature before writing code. Runs a structured discovery interview, then produces a design brief that guides implementation. Use during the planning phase to establish design direction, constraints, and strategy before any code is written.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- impeccable: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context. Call with 'craft' for shape-then-build, 'teach' for design context setup, or 'extract' to pull reusable components and tokens into the design system.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- layout: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- dbg: Debug applications using the dbg CLI debugger. Supports Node.js (V8/CDP), Bun (WebKit/JSC), and native code via LLDB (DAP). Use when: (1) investigating runtime bugs by stepping through code, (2) inspecting variable values at specific execution points, (3) setting breakpoints and conditional breakpoints, (4) evaluating expressions in a paused context, (5) hot-patching code without restarting (JS/TS), (6) debugging test failures by attaching to a running process, (7) debugging C/C++/Rust/Swift with LLDB, (8) any task where understanding runtime behavior requires a debugger. Triggers: "debug this", "set a breakpoint", "step through", "inspect variables", "why is this value wrong", "trace execution", "attach debugger", "runtime error", "segfault", "core dump".
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, automated anti-pattern detection, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- tech-debt: Create a new technical debt entry in the tech-debt/ directory. Use when identifying technical debt to formalize, track, or document for future resolution.
- improve-ai-harness: Analyze review feedbacks or development friction points and suggest improvements to the AI harness (rules, CLAUDE.md, docs, tooling). Use when asked to improve AI rules, after a code review with issues, or to strengthen the AI configuration.
- roadmap-feature: Create a new epic entry in the roadmap/ directory. Use when the user wants to plan a new feature, add an item to the roadmap, or formalize an epic for future implementation.
- session-save: Save the current session state — decisions, modified files, current status, and next steps — to a handoff file for later resume.
- learn-from-mistakes: Now that you learnt some new stuff, write detailed specs and reduce ambiguity before handing work...
- commit: Commit les changements du workspace git avec guidelines opinionated (conventional commits détectés, langue détectée, fixup automatique)
- pr-context: Load the full context of a GitHub Pull Request so the user can ask questions about it.
- log-4sh-time:log-4sh-time: Interactive workflow to log work time in 4track with semantic task matching. View aggregated work statistics by project, perimeter, and task. Use this skill when the user wants to create a worklog, log hours, track time, record work activities, or analyze time spent on tasks.
- typescript-standards:enforce-ts-standards: Enforce type-safe TypeScript patterns with Zod validation, pattern matching, and discriminated unions to prevent runtime errors. Use when writing TypeScript code, reviewing for type safety, designing data validation flows, or refactoring to eliminate impossible states.

using it. Fetches its source code, scans for malicious or suspicious patterns
(outbound network calls, telemetry, eval/exec, install hooks, opaque file writes,
supply-chain risks), then recursively audits same-author dependencies found in the

1	name: Release
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    tags:
6	      - 'v*.*.*'
7	
8	jobs:
9	  release:
10	    name: Build and Release
11	    runs-on: macos-15
12	    timeout-minutes: 30
13	    permissions:
14	      contents: write
15	
16	    steps:
17	      - name: Checkout
18	        uses: actions/checkout@v4
19	
20	      - name: Select Xcode 26
21	        # Swift 6.2 (Xcode 26) is required because the codebase uses stable
22	        # @MainActor deinit, which is experimental in Swift 6.1 (Xcode 16.x).
23	        run: |
24	          XCODE_PATH=$(ls -d /Applications/Xcode_26*.app 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
25	          if [ -z "$XCODE_PATH" ]; then
26	            echo "No Xcode 26.x found on runner. Available:"
27	            ls -d /Applications/Xcode*.app
28	            exit 1
29	          fi
30	          echo "Using: $XCODE_PATH"
31	          sudo xcode-select -s "$XCODE_PATH/Contents/Developer"
32	
33	      - name: Show Build Environment
34	        run: |
35	          xcodebuild -version
36	          swift --version
37	          echo "macOS: $(sw_vers -productVersion)"
38	          echo "Tag: ${{ github.ref_name }}"
39	
40	      - name: Extract version from tag
41	        id: version
42	        run: |
43	          VERSION="${{ github.ref_name }}"
44	          VERSION="${VERSION#v}"
45	          echo "version=${VERSION}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
46	          echo "Version: ${VERSION}"
47	
48	      - name: Strip SwiftLint plugin for CI build
49	        run: |
50	          # SwiftLintPlugins fails on CI runners with "prebuild command cannot
51	          # use executables built from source". Lint is a dev/PR concern, not
52	          # a release concern — strip the plugin so the release build proceeds.
53	          python3 -c "
54	          import re, pathlib
55	          p = pathlib.Path('AIUsagesTrackers/Package.swift')
56	          s = p.read_text()
57	          s = re.sub(r',\s*plugins:\s*\[[^\]]*\]', '', s)
58	          p.write_text(s)
59	          "
60	          echo "--- Patched Package.swift ---"
61	          cat AIUsagesTrackers/Package.swift
62	
63	      - name: Build app bundle
64	        env:
65	          BUNDLE_VERSION: ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}
66	        run: |
67	          ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
68	          echo "✅ Build completed"
69	
70	      - name: Create release artifact
71	        id: artifact
72	        run: |
73	          cd dist
74	          ditto -c -k --keepParent "AI Usages Tracker.app" "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip"
75	          shasum -a 256 "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip" > "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip.sha256"
76	          echo "SHA256:"
77	          cat "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip.sha256"
78	
79	      - name: Create GitHub Release
80	        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
81	        with:
82	          name: ${{ github.ref_name }}
83	          draft: false
84	          generate_release_notes: true
85	          files: |
86	            dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip
87	            dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip.sha256
88	        env:
89	          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
90	
91	      - name: Compute SHA256 for Homebrew
92	        id: sha
93	        run: |
94	          SHA=$(shasum -a 256 dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip | awk '{print $1}')
95	          echo "sha256=${SHA}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
96	          echo "SHA256: ${SHA}"
97	
98	      - name: Summary
99	        run: |
100	          echo "🎉 Release ${{ github.ref_name }} published"
101	          echo ""
102	          echo "⚠️  Ad-hoc signed build (no Apple notarization)"
103	          echo "   First launch: right-click > Open to bypass Gatekeeper"
104	
105	    outputs:
106	      version: ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}
107	      sha256: ${{ steps.sha.outputs.sha256 }}
108	
109	  update-tap:
110	    name: Update Homebrew Tap
111	    needs: release
112	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
113	    steps:
114	      - name: Checkout homebrew-tap
115	        uses: actions/checkout@v4
116	        with:
117	          repository: fcamblor/homebrew-tap
118	          token: ${{ secrets.HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN }}
119	
120	      - name: Update cask file
121	        run: |
122	          CASK="Casks/ai-usages-tracker.rb"
123	          [ -f "$CASK" ] || { echo "$CASK missing in tap"; exit 1; }
124	
125	          sed -i "s/version \".*\"/version \"${{ needs.release.outputs.version }}\"/" "$CASK"
126	          sed -i "s/sha256 \".*\"/sha256 \"${{ needs.release.outputs.sha256 }}\"/" "$CASK"
127	
128	          grep -q "version \"${{ needs.release.outputs.version }}\"" "$CASK"
129	          grep -q "sha256 \"${{ needs.release.outputs.sha256 }}\"" "$CASK"
130	
131	          echo "--- Updated cask ---"
132	          cat "$CASK"
133	
134	      - name: Commit and push
135	        run: |
136	          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
137	          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
138	          git add Casks/ai-usages-tracker.rb
139	          git commit -m "chore: bump ai-usages-tracker to ${{ needs.release.outputs.version }}"
140	          git push

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - 'v*.*.*'

jobs:
  release:
    name: Build and Release
    runs-on: macos-15
    timeout-minutes: 30
    permissions:
      contents: write

    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Select Xcode 26
        # Swift 6.2 (Xcode 26) is required because the codebase uses stable
        # @MainActor deinit, which is experimental in Swift 6.1 (Xcode 16.x).
        run: |
          XCODE_PATH=$(ls -d /Applications/Xcode_26*.app 2>/dev/null | sort -V | tail -1)
          if [ -z "$XCODE_PATH" ]; then
            echo "No Xcode 26.x found on runner. Available:"
            ls -d /Applications/Xcode*.app
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Using: $XCODE_PATH"
          sudo xcode-select -s "$XCODE_PATH/Contents/Developer"

      - name: Show Build Environment
        run: |
          xcodebuild -version
          swift --version
          echo "macOS: $(sw_vers -productVersion)"
          echo "Tag: ${{ github.ref_name }}"

      - name: Extract version from tag
        id: version
        run: |
          VERSION="${{ github.ref_name }}"
          VERSION="${VERSION#v}"
          echo "version=${VERSION}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Version: ${VERSION}"

      - name: Strip SwiftLint plugin for CI build
        run: |
          # SwiftLintPlugins fails on CI runners with "prebuild command cannot
          # use executables built from source". Lint is a dev/PR concern, not
          # a release concern — strip the plugin so the release build proceeds.
          python3 -c "
          import re, pathlib
          p = pathlib.Path('AIUsagesTrackers/Package.swift')
          s = p.read_text()
          s = re.sub(r',\s*plugins:\s*\[[^\]]*\]', '', s)
          p.write_text(s)
          "
          echo "--- Patched Package.swift ---"
          cat AIUsagesTrackers/Package.swift

      - name: Build app bundle
        env:
          BUNDLE_VERSION: ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}
        run: |
          ./scripts/build-app-bundle.sh
          echo "✅ Build completed"

      - name: Create release artifact
        id: artifact
        run: |
          cd dist
          ditto -c -k --keepParent "AI Usages Tracker.app" "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip"
          shasum -a 256 "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip" > "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip.sha256"
          echo "SHA256:"
          cat "AI-Usages-Tracker.zip.sha256"

      - name: Create GitHub Release
        uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          name: ${{ github.ref_name }}
          draft: false
          generate_release_notes: true
          files: |
            dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip
            dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip.sha256
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Compute SHA256 for Homebrew
        id: sha
        run: |
          SHA=$(shasum -a 256 dist/AI-Usages-Tracker.zip | awk '{print $1}')
          echo "sha256=${SHA}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "SHA256: ${SHA}"

      - name: Summary
        run: |
          echo "🎉 Release ${{ github.ref_name }} published"
          echo ""
          echo "⚠️  Ad-hoc signed build (no Apple notarization)"
          echo "   First launch: right-click > Open to bypass Gatekeeper"

    outputs:
      version: ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}
      sha256: ${{ steps.sha.outputs.sha256 }}

  update-tap:
    name: Update Homebrew Tap
    needs: release
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout homebrew-tap
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          repository: fcamblor/homebrew-tap
          token: ${{ secrets.HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN }}

      - name: Update cask file
        run: |
          CASK="Casks/ai-usages-tracker.rb"
          [ -f "$CASK" ] || { echo "$CASK missing in tap"; exit 1; }

          sed -i "s/version \".*\"/version \"${{ needs.release.outputs.version }}\"/" "$CASK"
          sed -i "s/sha256 \".*\"/sha256 \"${{ needs.release.outputs.sha256 }}\"/" "$CASK"

          grep -q "version \"${{ needs.release.outputs.version }}\"" "$CASK"
          grep -q "sha256 \"${{ needs.release.outputs.sha256 }}\"" "$CASK"

          echo "--- Updated cask ---"
          cat "$CASK"

      - name: Commit and push
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git add Casks/ai-usages-tracker.rb
          git commit -m "chore: bump ai-usages-tracker to ${{ needs.release.outputs.version }}"
          git push

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# Build a distributable .app bundle for the menubar app.
# Output: dist/AI Usages Tracker.app (ad-hoc signed, unsealed).
#
set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}")" && pwd)"
REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
PACKAGE_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/AIUsagesTrackers"
DIST_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/dist"

APP_DISPLAY_NAME="AI Usages Tracker"
APP_BINARY_NAME="AIUsagesTrackers"
BUNDLE_ID="io.github.fcamblor.ai-usages-tracker"
BUNDLE_VERSION="${BUNDLE_VERSION:-0.1.0}"
MIN_MACOS_VERSION="14.0"

APP_BUNDLE="$DIST_DIR/$APP_DISPLAY_NAME.app"
CONTENTS="$APP_BUNDLE/Contents"
MACOS_DIR="$CONTENTS/MacOS"
RESOURCES_DIR="$CONTENTS/Resources"
BUILD_CONFIG="release"
BUILD_DIR="$PACKAGE_DIR/.build/$BUILD_CONFIG"

echo "→ Building release binaries"
cd "$PACKAGE_DIR"
swift build -c "$BUILD_CONFIG" --product "$APP_BINARY_NAME"
swift build -c "$BUILD_CONFIG" --product IconExporter

# Resolve absolute build paths (swift emits a symlink in some configurations).
APP_BIN="$(swift build -c "$BUILD_CONFIG" --show-bin-path)/$APP_BINARY_NAME"
ICON_EXPORTER_BIN="$(swift build -c "$BUILD_CONFIG" --show-bin-path)/IconExporter"

echo "→ Assembling bundle at $APP_BUNDLE"
rm -rf "$APP_BUNDLE"
mkdir -p "$MACOS_DIR" "$RESOURCES_DIR"

echo "→ Rendering .icns from AppIconView"
ICONSET_TMP="$(mktemp -d)/AppIcon.iconset"
mkdir -p "$ICONSET_TMP"
"$ICON_EXPORTER_BIN" "$ICONSET_TMP"
iconutil -c icns "$ICONSET_TMP" -o "$RESOURCES_DIR/AppIcon.icns"

echo "→ Copying executable"
cp "$APP_BIN" "$MACOS_DIR/$APP_BINARY_NAME"
chmod +x "$MACOS_DIR/$APP_BINARY_NAME"

# SwiftPM emits a resource bundle next to the binary for any target declaring
# `resources:`. The generated `Bundle.module` accessor looks for it relative to
# the executable URL, so it must sit alongside the binary in Contents/MacOS —
# not in Contents/Resources. Without this, the app fatal-errors on first access.
echo "→ Copying SwiftPM resource bundles"
BIN_PATH="$(swift build -c "$BUILD_CONFIG" --show-bin-path)"
shopt -s nullglob
for bundle in "$BIN_PATH"/*.bundle; do
  bundle_name="$(basename "$bundle")"
  echo "  • $bundle_name"
  cp -R "$bundle" "$MACOS_DIR/"
  # SwiftPM emits a flat bundle (resources at the root, no Info.plist).
  # codesign rejects that as "bundle format unrecognized" — give it a minimal
  # Info.plist so the embedded bundle can be signed with the parent .app.
  bundle_basename="${bundle_name%.bundle}"
  cat > "$MACOS_DIR/$bundle_name/Info.plist" <<PLIST
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>CFBundleIdentifier</key>
  <string>$BUNDLE_ID.resources.$bundle_basename</string>
  <key>CFBundleName</key>
  <string>$bundle_basename</string>
  <key>CFBundlePackageType</key>
  <string>BNDL</string>
  <key>CFBundleShortVersionString</key>
  <string>$BUNDLE_VERSION</string>
  <key>CFBundleVersion</key>
  <string>$BUNDLE_VERSION</string>
</dict>
</plist>
PLIST
done
shopt -u nullglob

echo "→ Writing Info.plist"
cat > "$CONTENTS/Info.plist" <<PLIST
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>CFBundleName</key>
  <string>$APP_DISPLAY_NAME</string>
  <key>CFBundleDisplayName</key>
  <string>$APP_DISPLAY_NAME</string>
  <key>CFBundleExecutable</key>
  <string>$APP_BINARY_NAME</string>
  <key>CFBundleIdentifier</key>
  <string>$BUNDLE_ID</string>
  <key>CFBundlePackageType</key>
  <string>APPL</string>
  <key>CFBundleShortVersionString</key>
  <string>$BUNDLE_VERSION</string>
  <key>CFBundleVersion</key>
  <string>$BUNDLE_VERSION</string>
  <key>CFBundleIconFile</key>
  <string>AppIcon</string>
  <key>LSMinimumSystemVersion</key>
  <string>$MIN_MACOS_VERSION</string>
  <key>LSUIElement</key>
  <true/>
  <key>NSHighResolutionCapable</key>
  <true/>
  <key>NSHumanReadableCopyright</key>
  <string>© $(date +%Y) Frédéric Camblor</string>
  <key>NSAppleEventsUsageDescription</key>
  <string>AI Usages Tracker checks System Events for legacy login items so toggling "Launch at login" stays consistent and avoids registering duplicate entries.</string>
</dict>
</plist>
PLIST

echo "→ Ad-hoc code-signing (required for Gatekeeper on unsigned binaries)"
codesign --force --deep --sign - "$APP_BUNDLE"

echo
echo "✓ Bundle ready: $APP_BUNDLE"
echo "  Launch with: open \"$APP_BUNDLE\""

content.content:
1	# Architecture
2	
3	An executable Swift Package produces a **menubar-only** macOS application: no Dock icon, no main window, and all interaction flows through a single menu bar item.
4	
5	Invariants worth preserving when modifying the codebase:
6	
7	- The app sets its activation policy to `.accessory` at startup. Removing this call makes macOS show a Dock icon and breaks the menubar-only contract.
8	- The UI is exposed through an AppKit `NSStatusItem` owned by `AppDelegate`, not SwiftUI's `MenuBarExtra`. `MenuBarExtra` was dropped after it proved unable to render per-segment colored indicators (template tinting strips colors, mixed HStacks silently truncate). See `docs/SWIFT-MENUBAR.md` before changing anything menu-bar related. User-visible features belong inside the popover's root SwiftUI view — there is intentionally no main `WindowGroup`.
9	
10	## Package structure
11	
12	The Swift package is split into a library target and an executable target, plus a test target that exercises the library. The library contains all domain logic; the executable is a thin SwiftUI entry point.
13	
14	## Usage-fetching pipeline
15	
16	A `UsageConnector` protocol abstracts vendor-specific API access. Each connector resolves an active account and fetches usage data asynchronously. A connector may also emit additional optional per-model `timeWindow` entries when the upstream API includes them — these travel through the same persistence and display pipeline as the mandatory session and weekly aggregates, and are silently omitted when the API does not provide them. The first concrete implementation targets the Claude API via OAuth tokens stored in the macOS Keychain.
17	
18	A polling actor periodically invokes all registered connectors in parallel and merges results into a shared JSON file.
19	
20	## Persistence
21	
22	Usage data is persisted as a JSON file at `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usages.json`. The schema is a top-level `usages` array where each entry is keyed by `(vendor, account)`. A dedicated actor (`UsagesFileManager`) serializes all reads and writes for internal callers. Writes use the system's atomic write facility to prevent partial-file corruption. All public methods also acquire an advisory POSIX `flock` on a dedicated lock file (`usages.json.lock`) before touching the data file, so external readers (widgets, scripts) that do the same can safely coordinate access.
23	
24	History is persisted separately as append-only JSONL snapshots under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usage-history/YYYY/MM/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl`, partitioned by UTC date. `SnapshotRecorder` writes one `TickSnapshot` line only when the flattened account/metric payload changes. Expired time-window metrics are recorded as null values, which lets chart rendering break lines across reset or missing-data boundaries instead of interpolating through them.
25	
26	## Display pipeline
27	
28	A file watcher observes `usages.json` using a hybrid strategy: a kernel `DispatchSource` fires on `.write`/`.delete`/`.rename` events, backed by a polling timer that re-checks on a configurable polling interval to handle atomic replaces and network-mount edge cases. Events are debounced to coalesce rapid successive writes into a single read.
29	
30	An `@Observable` store (running on `@MainActor`) receives each new file snapshot, decodes it, tracks active accounts, and resolves the user's configured menu bar segments. A secondary countdown timer periodically refreshes the remaining-time values in the display so they stay current between file changes. When data is unavailable or malformed the label falls back to `"--"`.
31	
32	When the user clicks the menu bar item, `AppDelegate` toggles an `NSPopover` hosting the root SwiftUI view via `NSHostingController`. The popover renders one card per vendor/account entry, sorted alphabetically by vendor then by account (active accounts first within a vendor). Each card shows time-window metrics (gauge bar, theoretical pace marker, remaining time, next reset date) and pay-as-you-go metrics (amount with currency). An empty state is shown when no entries are available. A footer provides the app name and a Quit shortcut.
33	
34	The menu bar label itself is rasterised into a non-template `NSImage` by `MenuBarLabelRenderer`, so per-tier dot colors survive macOS's template tinting. Text color is picked from the status button's `effectiveAppearance` (which tracks the menu bar, not the app) and re-rendered whenever that appearance changes.
35	
36	## Usage history charts
37	
38	`UsageHistoryReader` recursively reads the JSONL history, filters points to the selected window, skips malformed lines, ignores future points, and reports whether older or newer data exists outside the current window for chart navigation. Decoded files are cached by modification date and size to keep repeated chart refreshes cheap.
39	
40	The history tab renders the user's `ChartConfiguration` list. Each chart is resolved through `ChartSeriesResolver`: `allAvailable` groups every history series with at least one non-null point, while `custom` follows the configured vendor/account/metric series and applies per-series label, color, and line-style preferences. A custom series targeting "currently active account" is resolved against the live store entries at render time.
41	
42	Null history points are kept until rendering. The chart panel omits them from `LineMark` values but uses them to increment segment identifiers, so Swift Charts draws separate line segments around reset gaps. Hover tooltips use pre-grouped series data and a nearest-point binary search rather than scanning every point on every hover event.
43	
44	## Account monitoring
45	
46	A separate monitoring actor polls the vendor's local config file at a short fixed interval to detect account switches in real time. When a switch is detected, it updates the `isActive` flag on the corresponding persistence entry without waiting for the next usage fetch. This separation keeps account-status latency low without coupling it to the (slower) API polling cadence.
47	
48	## Preferences
49	
50	An injectable preferences protocol backed by `UserDefaults` is the single source of truth for user-adjustable runtime behaviour. Consumers include the polling actor (refresh cadence), logging subsystem (verbosity), menu bar segment resolver, and history chart renderer. Changes made in the settings window take effect immediately — the poller re-reads the interval on every tick, the logger resolves its effective level on every log call, and SwiftUI observes the menu bar and chart configuration lists.
51	
52	The settings window is a standard SwiftUI `Settings` scene (tabbed, HIG chrome, `Cmd+,`). Because the app uses `.accessory` activation policy, opening the window requires explicitly activating the app before sending the `showSettingsWindow:` action.
53	
54	Launch-at-login state is reconciled at startup: if the user toggled the entry in System Settings > Login Items, the preferences store is updated to match the system state rather than overriding it.
55	
56	## Logging
57	
58	Two log files live under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/`: one for the app lifecycle and poller events, another for connector-specific activity. Log level is configurable via the `AI_TRACKER_LOG_LEVEL` environment variable; when that variable is absent, the level falls back to the user's setting in the preferences window (default: info). Size-based rotation keeps each file under 5 MB with one backup. A retention purge removes entries older than 7 days from all managed log files at startup and every 24 hours, using atomic rewrites so a crash mid-cleanup leaves the previous file intact.
59	
60	For authoritative details (Swift tools version, platform minimums, target layout), read the package manifest rather than mirroring them here.
61

# Architecture

An executable Swift Package produces a **menubar-only** macOS application: no Dock icon, no main window, and all interaction flows through a single menu bar item.

Invariants worth preserving when modifying the codebase:

- The app sets its activation policy to `.accessory` at startup. Removing this call makes macOS show a Dock icon and breaks the menubar-only contract.
- The UI is exposed through an AppKit `NSStatusItem` owned by `AppDelegate`, not SwiftUI's `MenuBarExtra`. `MenuBarExtra` was dropped after it proved unable to render per-segment colored indicators (template tinting strips colors, mixed HStacks silently truncate). See `docs/SWIFT-MENUBAR.md` before changing anything menu-bar related. User-visible features belong inside the popover's root SwiftUI view — there is intentionally no main `WindowGroup`.

## Package structure

The Swift package is split into a library target and an executable target, plus a test target that exercises the library. The library contains all domain logic; the executable is a thin SwiftUI entry point.

## Usage-fetching pipeline

A `UsageConnector` protocol abstracts vendor-specific API access. Each connector resolves an active account and fetches usage data asynchronously. A connector may also emit additional optional per-model `timeWindow` entries when the upstream API includes them — these travel through the same persistence and display pipeline as the mandatory session and weekly aggregates, and are silently omitted when the API does not provide them. The first concrete implementation targets the Claude API via OAuth tokens stored in the macOS Keychain.

A polling actor periodically invokes all registered connectors in parallel and merges results into a shared JSON file.

## Persistence

Usage data is persisted as a JSON file at `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usages.json`. The schema is a top-level `usages` array where each entry is keyed by `(vendor, account)`. A dedicated actor (`UsagesFileManager`) serializes all reads and writes for internal callers. Writes use the system's atomic write facility to prevent partial-file corruption. All public methods also acquire an advisory POSIX `flock` on a dedicated lock file (`usages.json.lock`) before touching the data file, so external readers (widgets, scripts) that do the same can safely coordinate access.

History is persisted separately as append-only JSONL snapshots under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/usage-history/YYYY/MM/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl`, partitioned by UTC date. `SnapshotRecorder` writes one `TickSnapshot` line only when the flattened account/metric payload changes. Expired time-window metrics are recorded as null values, which lets chart rendering break lines across reset or missing-data boundaries instead of interpolating through them.

## Display pipeline

A file watcher observes `usages.json` using a hybrid strategy: a kernel `DispatchSource` fires on `.write`/`.delete`/`.rename` events, backed by a polling timer that re-checks on a configurable polling interval to handle atomic replaces and network-mount edge cases. Events are debounced to coalesce rapid successive writes into a single read.

An `@Observable` store (running on `@MainActor`) receives each new file snapshot, decodes it, tracks active accounts, and resolves the user's configured menu bar segments. A secondary countdown timer periodically refreshes the remaining-time values in the display so they stay current between file changes. When data is unavailable or malformed the label falls back to `"--"`.

When the user clicks the menu bar item, `AppDelegate` toggles an `NSPopover` hosting the root SwiftUI view via `NSHostingController`. The popover renders one card per vendor/account entry, sorted alphabetically by vendor then by account (active accounts first within a vendor). Each card shows time-window metrics (gauge bar, theoretical pace marker, remaining time, next reset date) and pay-as-you-go metrics (amount with currency). An empty state is shown when no entries are available. A footer provides the app name and a Quit shortcut.

The menu bar label itself is rasterised into a non-template `NSImage` by `MenuBarLabelRenderer`, so per-tier dot colors survive macOS's template tinting. Text color is picked from the status button's `effectiveAppearance` (which tracks the menu bar, not the app) and re-rendered whenever that appearance changes.

## Usage history charts

`UsageHistoryReader` recursively reads the JSONL history, filters points to the selected window, skips malformed lines, ignores future points, and reports whether older or newer data exists outside the current window for chart navigation. Decoded files are cached by modification date and size to keep repeated chart refreshes cheap.

The history tab renders the user's `ChartConfiguration` list. Each chart is resolved through `ChartSeriesResolver`: `allAvailable` groups every history series with at least one non-null point, while `custom` follows the configured vendor/account/metric series and applies per-series label, color, and line-style preferences. A custom series targeting "currently active account" is resolved against the live store entries at render time.

Null history points are kept until rendering. The chart panel omits them from `LineMark` values but uses them to increment segment identifiers, so Swift Charts draws separate line segments around reset gaps. Hover tooltips use pre-grouped series data and a nearest-point binary search rather than scanning every point on every hover event.

## Account monitoring

A separate monitoring actor polls the vendor's local config file at a short fixed interval to detect account switches in real time. When a switch is detected, it updates the `isActive` flag on the corresponding persistence entry without waiting for the next usage fetch. This separation keeps account-status latency low without coupling it to the (slower) API polling cadence.

## Preferences

An injectable preferences protocol backed by `UserDefaults` is the single source of truth for user-adjustable runtime behaviour. Consumers include the polling actor (refresh cadence), logging subsystem (verbosity), menu bar segment resolver, and history chart renderer. Changes made in the settings window take effect immediately — the poller re-reads the interval on every tick, the logger resolves its effective level on every log call, and SwiftUI observes the menu bar and chart configuration lists.

The settings window is a standard SwiftUI `Settings` scene (tabbed, HIG chrome, `Cmd+,`). Because the app uses `.accessory` activation policy, opening the window requires explicitly activating the app before sending the `showSettingsWindow:` action.

Launch-at-login state is reconciled at startup: if the user toggled the entry in System Settings > Login Items, the preferences store is updated to match the system state rather than overriding it.

## Logging

Two log files live under `~/.cache/ai-usages-tracker/`: one for the app lifecycle and poller events, another for connector-specific activity. Log level is configurable via the `AI_TRACKER_LOG_LEVEL` environment variable; when that variable is absent, the level falls back to the user's setting in the preferences window (default: info). Size-based rotation keeps each file under 5 MB with one backup. A retention purge removes entries older than 7 days from all managed log files at startup and every 24 hours, using atomic rewrites so a crash mid-cleanup leaves the previous file intact.

For authoritative details (Swift tools version, platform minimums, target layout), read the package manifest rather than mirroring them here.

# Markdown authoring rules

## Language: English only

All Markdown files in this repository MUST be written in English, regardless of the conversation language. This includes `CLAUDE.md`, every file under `docs/`, `README.md`, and any new `*.md`.

- When creating a new Markdown file, write it in English from the start even if the user's prompt is in another language.
- When editing an existing Markdown file that contains non-English prose, translate the edited sections to English instead of preserving mixed languages.
- Code blocks, file paths, commands, and identifiers keep their original form — this rule applies to natural-language prose only.

## Write for longevity

Markdown in this repository is long-lived context. Optimize each file so it does not need to be rewritten as the code evolves. Avoid anything that ties documentation to a specific moment in time or to renamable artifacts:

- Do NOT reference specific commit SHAs, PR numbers, or branch names.
- Do NOT describe the project as being in a transient state ("currently a Hello World", "not yet implemented", "WIP"). If a feature does not exist, simply do not document it.
- Do NOT speculate about intent or future direction ("the name suggests…", "will eventually…"). Document what the code actually does today in terms that remain true tomorrow.
- Do NOT list every file, symbol, or dependency when a structural description is enough — exhaustive lists drift out of date quickly.
- Avoid naming specific files, types, or symbols that could be renamed or moved. Prefer describing roles and responsibilities ("the SwiftUI entry point", "the package manifest") over fixed identifiers. When a path is truly stable because a tool mandates it (e.g. `Package.swift` for Swift Package Manager, `.claude/rules/` for Claude Code), it is fine to use it.
- Do NOT duplicate information that already lives authoritatively in code or tool manifests (version numbers, platform minimums, target lists). Point readers at the source of truth instead of mirroring it.

When in doubt, ask: "Will this sentence still be accurate after the next three features ship, or after a plausible rename?" If not, rephrase or remove it.

## Single source of truth across Markdown files

When two Markdown files cover the same topic, exactly one is the authoritative spec; the other references it and adds only what is genuinely its own (workflow steps, scaffolding procedure, agent phases, etc.).

This applies in particular to these pairs:

- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (skill that implements it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/agents/<name>.md` (agent prompt that uses it)
- `docs/<topic>.md` (spec) ↔ `.claude/commands/<name>.md` (slash command that triggers it)

Rules:

- Templates, file formats, naming conventions, and directory layouts belong in the spec, NOT in the operational artifact.
- The operational artifact MUST start with a one-line pointer ("Read `docs/<topic>.md` first; it defines X, Y, Z.") and MUST include a conflict-resolution line ("If this artifact disagrees with `docs/<topic>.md`, the spec wins.").
- Before adding concrete content (a template block, a format string, a list of sections) to an operational artifact, check whether that content already exists in a `docs/` spec. If it does, reference it instead of copying it.

## Exception: `roadmap/`

Files under `roadmap/` intentionally describe planned, not-yet-implemented work. The "Write for longevity" rules above (no transient state, no speculation about future direction) do NOT apply there — a roadmap is precisely a record of intent and ordering that will evolve as features ship. The English-only rule still applies. See `docs/ROADMAP.md` for the roadmap file conventions.

235	    private func openSettings() {
236	        popover?.performClose(nil)

238	        // unless we temporarily switch to .regular so AppKit can make the window key.
239	        NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.regular)

242	        // SwiftUI creates the Settings window lazily on first showSettingsWindow: —
243	        // if it already exists, reuse it directly to avoid responder-chain races
244	        // that occur during the popover-dismissal + activation-policy transition.
245	        if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
246	            settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
247	        } else {
248	            // First-time path: AppKit needs a runloop tick after the .accessory →
249	            // .regular transition to install the standard App menu (which is where
250	            // SwiftUI wires the Settings… action). sendAction with a nil target
251	            // relies on that wiring, so we trigger the menu item directly once
252	            // AppKit has had a chance to build it.
253	            DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.15) {
254	                Self.triggerSettingsMenuItem()
255	                if let settingsWindow = Self.findSettingsWindow() {
256	                    settingsWindow.makeKeyAndOrderFront(nil)
257	                }
258	            }
259	        }
260	
261	        // Restore .accessory policy once the settings window is closed.
262	        if settingsWindowObserver == nil {
263	            // Use selector-based observer so the callback runs on @MainActor naturally,
264	            // avoiding Sendable-crossing issues with block-based addObserver(queue:).
265	            settingsWindowObserver = self
266	            NotificationCenter.default.addObserver(
267	                self,
268	                selector: #selector(handleSettingsWindowWillClose(_:)),
269	                name: NSWindow.willCloseNotification,
270	                object: nil
271	            )
272	        }
273	    }

275	    @objc private func handleSettingsWindowWillClose(_ notification: Notification) {
276	        // Only react to the Settings window closing — popovers and other
277	        // transient windows also emit willClose.
278	        guard let closingWindow = notification.object as? NSWindow else { return }
279	        let windowIdentifier = closingWindow.identifier?.rawValue ?? ""
280	        guard windowIdentifier.contains("Settings") || windowIdentifier.contains("Preferences") else { return }
281	        // willClose fires while the window is still visible and before
282	        // AppKit tears it down; defer to the next runloop tick so the
283	        // activation-policy change isn't overridden by pending AppKit work.
284	        DispatchQueue.main.async { [weak self] in
285	            NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.accessory)
286	            // Accessory transition doesn't always remove the app from the
287	            // Cmd+Tab / app-switcher list until another app is activated.
288	            if let frontmost = NSWorkspace.shared.runningApplications.first(where: {
289	                $0.isActive && $0.processIdentifier != ProcessInfo.processInfo.processIdentifier
290	            }) {
291	                frontmost.activate()
292	            } else {
293	                NSApp.hide(nil)
294	            }
295	            guard let self else { return }
296	            NotificationCenter.default.removeObserver(self, name: NSWindow.willCloseNotification, object: nil)
297	            self.settingsWindowObserver = nil
298	        }
299	    }
300	
301	    /// SwiftUI tags its Settings window with an identifier containing "Settings"
302	    /// (or "Preferences" on older SDKs). Match on the stable identifier rather
303	    /// than localized titles.
304	    private static func findSettingsWindow() -> NSWindow? {
305	        NSApp.windows.first { window in
306	            let identifier = window.identifier?.rawValue ?? ""
307	            return identifier.contains("Settings") || identifier.contains("Preferences")
308	        }
309	    }
310	
311	    /// Walk the main menu looking for the App menu's Settings… (or Preferences…)
312	    /// item and invoke it. Going through the menu guarantees the action is
313	    /// routed to whichever responder SwiftUI wired up, instead of relying on
314	    /// `sendAction(_:to:from:)` to resolve a nil target in the responder chain
315	    /// — which is unreliable right after an activation-policy switch.
316	    private static func triggerSettingsMenuItem() {
317	        guard let mainMenu = NSApp.mainMenu else { return }
318	        for topItem in mainMenu.items {
319	            guard let submenu = topItem.submenu else { continue }
320	            for (index, item) in submenu.items.enumerated() {
321	                let title = item.title
322	                if title.hasPrefix("Settings") || title.hasPrefix("Preferences") {
323	                    submenu.performActionForItem(at: index)
324	                    return
325	                }
326	            }
327	        }
328	    }
329	
330	    // MARK: - Quit
331	
332	    private func quit() {
333	        let pollerRef = poller
334	        let schedulerRef = snapshotScheduler
335	        let monitorRef = accountMonitor
336	        let codexMonitorRef = codexMonitor
337	        Task {
338	            await pollerRef?.stop()
339	            await schedulerRef?.stop()
340	            await monitorRef?.stop()
341	            await codexMonitorRef?.stop()
342	        }
343	        usageStore?.stop()
344	        pidGuard?.release()
345	        NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil)
346	    }
347	}

Avant d'implémenter, laisse-moi te proposer une approche claire pour validation, puis je code.

## Proposition d'architecture

**Détection de mise à jour**
- Nouveau service `UpdateChecker` (actor injectable) qui interroge `GET https://api.github.com/repos/fcamblor/mac-ai-trackers/releases/latest` (ou le repo correct — à confirmer).
- Compare `tag_name` (sans `v`) à `CFBundleShortVersionString` lu depuis `Bundle.main.infoDictionary`.
- Cadence : check au démarrage + toutes les 6h (timer dans un `UpdateCheckScheduler`), via `URLSession` injectable. Pas d'API key nécessaire (60 req/h sans auth, largement suffisant).
- Stocke dans les préférences : `lastCheckedAt`, `latestKnownVersion`, `dismissedVersion` (pour ne pas re-notifier la même version après dismiss).

**Notification**
- Pas de notification système (UNUserNotificationCenter nécessite l'autorisation utilisateur, ad-hoc signed → friction). Plutôt :
  - Badge/dot dans le menu bar (intégré au `MenuBarLabelRenderer` via un nouveau préfixe optionnel).
  - Bandeau dans le popover (top du `UsageDetailsView`) avec « Mise à jour disponible v1.2.3 → [Installer] [Plus tard] ».
- Préférence dans Settings : « Vérifier les mises à jour automatiquement » (on/off) + bouton « Vérifier maintenant ».

**Installation en un clic**
- Asset attendu dans la release : `AI-Usages-Tracker.zip` (déjà publié par `release.yml`).
- Service `UpdateInstaller` :
  1. Télécharge le `.zip` dans `~/Library/Caches/ai-usages-tracker/updates/`.
  2. Vérifie le sha256 par rapport au fichier `.sha256` également publié.
  3. Décompresse via `ditto -x -k`.
  4. Remplace le bundle courant à `Bundle.main.bundleURL` via `replaceItemAt:withItemAt:` (atomique, respecte la règle SWIFT-IO-ROBUSTNESS).
  5. Relance l'app : `Process` qui exec `open` du nouveau bundle, puis `NSApp.terminate`.
- Important : si l'app tourne depuis `/Applications` (Homebrew cask), il faut les droits d'écriture. Brew installe en user-writable, donc OK pour le cas majoritaire. Sinon, fallback : ouvrir la page de release dans le navigateur.

**Tests requis (règle "every public method must have at least one test")**
- `UpdateChecker` : mock `URLSession`, parse OK, parse erreur, version égale/plus ancienne/plus récente.
- `UpdateInstaller` : mock `URLSession` et `FileManager`, vérif sha256, atomicité du remplacement.

**Question avant que je code**

1. **Repo GitHub** : c'est bien `fcamblor/mac-ai-trackers` ? (le tap est `fcamblor/homebrew-tap`, le workflow ne révèle pas le repo source)
2. **Stratégie de cadence** : check au démarrage + toutes les 6h te va, ou tu préfères autre chose (1×/jour, manuel uniquement) ?
3. **Notification UI** : bandeau dans le popover seul, ou aussi un indicateur visuel dans le menu bar (petit point coloré) ? Je penche pour bandeau popover uniquement (le menu bar a déjà beaucoup d'info), avec optionnellement un petit dot si tu veux.
4. **Release asset** : actuellement `release.yml` publie `AI-Usages-Tracker.zip` + `.sha256`. Je m'appuie dessus, OK ?
5. **Roadmap** : tu veux que je commence par créer un fichier `roadmap/update-checker.md` (process documenté dans `docs/ROADMAP.md`), ou j'attaque directement l'implémentation ?

Une fois ces points confirmés, je code l'ensemble (service + UI + tests + entrée Settings).

content.content:
1	# Swift concurrency safety
2	
3	## Never block the cooperative thread pool
4	
5	Swift concurrency uses a fixed-size thread pool. Blocking calls starve other tasks.
6	
7	- **Never** call `Process.waitUntilExit()` or any synchronous blocking API from an `async` context or inside an `actor`. Wrap blocking work in a `DispatchQueue` or `Task.detached` with a continuation.
8	- **Never** call `readDataToEndOfFile()` *after* `waitUntilExit()` — this deadlocks when the pipe buffer is full. Always read pipes *before* waiting for process termination.
9	
10	```swift
11	// BAD — blocks the cooperative thread pool
12	func run() async throws -> Data {
13	    process.launch()
14	    let data = pipe.fileHandleForReading.readDataToEndOfFile() // deadlock risk
15	    process.waitUntilExit() // blocks a cooperative thread
16	    return data
17	}
18	
19	// GOOD — async with continuation, read before wait
20	func run() async throws -> Data {
21	    return try await withCheckedThrowingContinuation { continuation in
22	        DispatchQueue.global().async {
23	            process.launch()
24	            let data = pipe.fileHandleForReading.readDataToEndOfFile()
25	            process.waitUntilExit()
26	            continuation.resume(returning: data)
27	        }
28	    }
29	}
30	```
31	
32	## NSFormatter and other non-thread-safe Foundation types
33	
34	`DateFormatter`, `ISO8601DateFormatter`, `NumberFormatter`, and similar `NSFormatter` subclasses are **not thread-safe**. If used from concurrent contexts:
35	
36	- Make them `static let` on a dedicated serial queue, OR
37	- Create a new instance each time (acceptable for cold paths), OR
38	- Use Swift's `nonisolated(unsafe)` only if you prove single-threaded access.
39	
40	Never share a formatter across threads without synchronization.
41	
42	```swift
43	// BAD — data race
44	actor MyActor {
45	    private let formatter = ISO8601DateFormatter()
46	    func format(_ date: Date) -> String {
47	        formatter.string(from: date) // races if called from nonisolated context
48	    }
49	}
50	
51	// GOOD — one instance per call (simple, safe)
52	func format(_ date: Date) -> String {
53	    let formatter = ISO8601DateFormatter()
54	    return formatter.string(from: date)
55	}
56	
57	// GOOD — static on serial queue (reusable, safe)
58	enum DateFormatting {
59	    private static let queue = DispatchQueue(label: "formatter")
60	    private static let formatter = ISO8601DateFormatter()
61	    static func format(_ date: Date) -> String {
62	        queue.sync { formatter.string(from: date) }
63	    }
64	}
65	```
66	
67	## Do not use `weak self` inside actors
68	
69	Actors are reference types with their own isolation. Using `[weak self]` in closures inside an actor is unnecessary and misleading — the actor manages its own lifetime. Use `[self]` or capture nothing.
70	
71	## Always add a timeout when launching external processes
72	
73	When spawning a `Process`, always set a timeout mechanism (e.g., `DispatchSource.makeTimerSource` or `Task.sleep` + cancellation). A hung child process must not hang the app forever.
74

1	# Swift value objects
2	
3	## Prefer value objects over primitive types for domain concepts
4	
5	Representing domain concepts as plain `String` or `Int` makes APIs ambiguous and allows values to be accidentally swapped. Introduce a value object (a struct wrapping a primitive) whenever a field has a distinct identity or constrained meaning.
6	
7	Typical candidates in this codebase:
8	- Email addresses, vendor names, ISO dates — wrap `String`
9	- Percentages, durations in a fixed unit — wrap `Int`
10	- Type discriminators with a closed set — use a `String` enum
11	
12	```swift
13	// BAD — impossible to tell which String is which at the call site
14	func updateIsActive(vendor: String, activeAccount: String?) { ... }
15	updateIsActive(vendor: "claude", activeAccount: "user@example.com")
16	
17	// GOOD — self-documenting, type-checked
18	func updateIsActive(vendor: Vendor, activeAccount: AccountEmail?) { ... }
19	updateIsActive(vendor: .claude, activeAccount: "user@example.com")
20	```
21	
22	## Use a struct, not an enum, for open-ended string domains
23	
24	Use a `struct` when new values can appear without a code change (e.g., vendor names, error codes). Name each known value as a `static let` constant. Unknown values decode transparently without throwing.
25	
26	```swift
27	public struct Vendor: RawRepresentable, Codable, Equatable, Hashable, Sendable {
28	    public let rawValue: String
29	    public init(rawValue: String) { self.rawValue = rawValue }
30	
31	    public static let claude = Vendor(rawValue: "claude")
32	}
33	```
34	
35	Use a `enum` only when the set is closed and an unknown value must be a decode error (e.g., a JSON discriminator).
36	
37	## Implement Codable manually — the default synthesis does not work for RawRepresentable structs
38	
39	`JSONEncoder`/`JSONDecoder` do not synthesise `Codable` conformance for custom structs that are `RawRepresentable`. Implement the two methods explicitly:
40	
41	```swift
42	public init(from decoder: Decoder) throws {
43	    rawValue = try decoder.singleValueContainer().decode(String.self)
44	}
45	
46	public func encode(to encoder: Encoder) throws {
47	    var container = encoder.singleValueContainer()
48	    try container.encode(rawValue)
49	}
50	```
51	
52	## Add ExpressibleByXxxLiteral for ergonomic call sites
53	
54	String-based value objects should conform to `ExpressibleByStringLiteral`; integer-based ones to `ExpressibleByIntegerLiteral`. This keeps call sites and tests readable without sacrificing type safety.
55	
56	```swift
57	extension AccountEmail: ExpressibleByStringLiteral {
58	    public init(stringLiteral value: String) { rawValue = value }
59	}
60	
61	// Callers and tests look the same as before
62	let entry = VendorUsageEntry(vendor: "claude", account: "user@example.com")
63	dict["user@example.com"]  // subscript resolves to AccountEmail via ExpressibleByStringLiteral
64	```
65	
66	Without this conformance, every call site needs an explicit `AccountEmail(rawValue: "...")` constructor — correct but noisy.
67	
68	## Add CustomStringConvertible for clean logging
69	
70	Value objects used in log messages should implement `CustomStringConvertible` and return `rawValue` as their description. Without this, string interpolation would print the struct's default representation (`Vendor(rawValue: "claude")` instead of `claude`).
71	
72	```swift
73	extension Vendor: CustomStringConvertible {
74	    public var description: String { rawValue }
75	}
76	```
77	
78	## Add domain behaviour as computed properties
79	
80	When a value object stores a string that needs parsing (e.g., an ISO date), expose a computed property rather than requiring callers to parse it themselves. This centralises the parsing logic.
81	
82	```swift
83	public struct ISODate: RawRepresentable, ... {
84	    public let rawValue: String
85	
86	    /// Returns nil if the stored string is not a parseable ISO 8601 date.
87	    public var date: Date? {
88	        // New formatter per call — ISO8601DateFormatter is not thread-safe; acceptable for this cold path
89	        ISO8601DateFormatter().date(from: rawValue)
90	    }
91	
92	    public init(date: Date) {
93	        // New formatter per call — acceptable for this cold path
94	        rawValue = ISO8601DateFormatter().string(from: date)
95	    }
96	}
97	```
98	
99	Do not share a static `ISO8601DateFormatter` from inside a value-type method; value types have no isolation boundary. Creating one per call is thread-safe and acceptable for cold paths (see `docs/SWIFT-CONCURRENCY.md`).
100	
101	## Add Comparable where ordering makes sense
102	
103	For quantity wrappers (`UsagePercent`, `DurationMinutes`), add `Comparable` by delegating to `rawValue`. This enables `min`, `max`, `sort`, and `<` comparisons in business logic.
104	
105	```swift
106	public static func < (lhs: UsagePercent, rhs: UsagePercent) -> Bool {
107	    lhs.rawValue < rhs.rawValue
108	}
109	```
110	
111	## Value object checklist
112	
113	When introducing a new domain field, ask:
114	
115	| Question | If yes |
116	|---|---|
117	| Can two fields of this type be accidentally swapped? | Wrap in a value object |
118	| Can new values appear at runtime without a code change? | Use a `struct`, not an `enum` |
119	| Is the raw value compared frequently in tests? | Add `ExpressibleByXxxLiteral` |
120	| Is the value used in log messages? | Add `CustomStringConvertible` |
121	| Does the raw value need parsing into another type? | Add a computed property |
122	| Does ordering between values make sense? | Conform to `Comparable` |
123

1	# Swift error handling
2	
3	## Never swallow errors with `try?` on fallible operations
4	
5	Using `try?` silently discards the error. This is only acceptable for truly optional operations (e.g., deleting a cache file). For any operation whose failure affects correctness, use `try` with explicit error handling.
6	
7	Critical operations that must NEVER use `try?`:
8	- `FileManager.createDirectory` — if it fails, all subsequent writes fail silently
9	- `FileManager.moveItem` / `copyItem` — rotation/backup failures mean data loss
10	- Network requests — silent failure means stale or missing data
11	- Keychain operations — silent failure means auth is broken
12	
13	```swift
14	// BAD — silent failure, subsequent code assumes directory exists
15	try? FileManager.default.createDirectory(at: logsDir, withIntermediateDirectories: true)
16	
17	// GOOD — propagate or handle explicitly
18	try FileManager.default.createDirectory(at: logsDir, withIntermediateDirectories: true)
19	```
20	
21	## Never log success after a `catch` without `return`/`throw`
22	
23	If a `catch` block logs an error but does not `return` or `throw`, execution falls through to subsequent code that may log success or assume the operation succeeded.
24	
25	```swift
26	// BAD — logs "saved" even on failure
27	do {
28	    try save(data)
29	} catch {
30	    logger.error("save failed: \(error)")
31	}
32	logger.info("saved successfully") // reached on failure!
33	
34	// GOOD — return in catch
35	do {
36	    try save(data)
37	    logger.info("saved successfully")
38	} catch {
39	    logger.error("save failed: \(error)")
40	    return
41	}
42	```
43	
44	## Error types must carry diagnostic context
45	
46	Custom error enums must include associated values with enough context to diagnose issues without access to logs.
47	
48	```swift
49	// BAD — caller cannot distinguish causes or log details
50	enum ConnectorError: Error {
51	    case authenticationFailed
52	    case networkError
53	}
54	
55	// GOOD — associated values carry context
56	enum ConnectorError: Error {
57	    case authenticationFailed(account: String, reason: String)
58	    case networkError(statusCode: Int, url: URL)
59	    case timeout(processPath: String, seconds: Int)
60	}
61	```
62	
63	## Distinguish error causes precisely
64	
65	When an operation can fail for multiple distinct reasons, create separate error cases. Do not conflate timeout, permission denial, and process failure into one case.
66

1	# Swift I/O robustness
2	
3	## Atomic file writes
4	
5	When writing data files (JSON, logs, config), always write to a temporary file first, then atomically rename. This prevents corruption if the process is killed mid-write.
6	
7	```swift
8	// BAD — partial write on crash
9	try data.write(to: targetURL)
10	
11	// GOOD — atomic rename
12	let tempURL = targetURL.appendingPathExtension("tmp")
13	try data.write(to: tempURL)
14	try FileManager.default.moveItem(at: tempURL, to: targetURL)
15	
16	// ALSO GOOD — Foundation's built-in atomic option
17	try data.write(to: targetURL, options: .atomic)
18	```
19	
20	## File locking: avoid TOCTOU and always set a timeout
21	
22	When using `flock()` or any advisory lock:
23	- Never check-then-act (TOCTOU). Acquire the lock, then perform the operation, then release.
24	- Always set a timeout on lock acquisition. A hung process holding a lock must not block all future runs forever.
25	- Use `defer` to guarantee unlock.
26	
27	```swift
28	// GOOD pattern
29	let fd = open(path, O_RDWR | O_CREAT, 0o644)
30	guard fd >= 0 else { throw IOError.cannotOpen(path) }
31	defer { close(fd) }
32	
33	// Non-blocking attempt with retry + timeout
34	let deadline = Date().addingTimeInterval(5.0)
35	while flock(fd, LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB) != 0 {
36	    guard Date() < deadline else { throw IOError.lockTimeout(path) }
37	    try await Task.sleep(for: .milliseconds(50))
38	}
39	defer { flock(fd, LOCK_UN) }
40	```
41	
42	## Efficient collection merges
43	
44	When merging two collections by a key, use a dictionary for O(n+m) lookup instead of nested loops O(n*m).
45	
46	```swift
47	// BAD — O(n*m)
48	for new in incoming {
49	    if let idx = existing.firstIndex(where: { $0.id == new.id }) {
50	        existing[idx] = new
51	    } else {
52	        existing.append(new)
53	    }
54	}
55	
56	// GOOD — O(n+m)
57	var indexById = Dictionary(uniqueKeysWithValues: existing.enumerated().map { ($0.element.id, $0.offset) })
58	for item in incoming {
59	    if let idx = indexById[item.id] {
60	        existing[idx] = item
61	    } else {
62	        indexById[item.id] = existing.count
63	        existing.append(item)
64	    }
65	}
66	```

1	# Swift testability and test coverage
2	
3	## Design for dependency injection from the start
4	
5	Every external dependency (network, file system, keychain, system clock, process execution) must be injectable via a protocol. This is non-negotiable for testability.
6	
7	```swift
8	// BAD — hard-coded dependency, untestable
9	actor ClaudeConnector {
10	    func fetch() async throws -> Data {
11	        let (data, _) = try await URLSession.shared.data(from: url)
12	        return data
13	    }
14	}
15	
16	// GOOD — injectable
17	protocol HTTPClient: Sendable {
18	    func data(from url: URL) async throws -> (Data, URLResponse)
19	}
20	
21	extension URLSession: HTTPClient {}
22	
23	actor ClaudeConnector {
24	    private let httpClient: HTTPClient
25	    init(httpClient: HTTPClient = URLSession.shared) {
26	        self.httpClient = httpClient
27	    }
28	}
29	```
30	
31	Apply this to: `URLSession`, `Keychain` access, `FileManager`, `Process` execution, `Date()` / clock.
32	
33	## Reuse expensive objects — do not instantiate per call
34	
35	`ISO8601DateFormatter`, `JSONDecoder`, `JSONEncoder`, and similar heavyweight objects must be created once and reused. Do not instantiate them inside functions called repeatedly.
36	
37	## Test every public method's primary path
38	
39	When creating a new type, every public method must have at least one test covering the success path. In particular:
40	- Network-fetching methods (`fetchUsages`, `fetchToken`, etc.)
41	- File I/O methods (write, read, rotate, merge)
42	- Lifecycle methods (start/stop idempotence)
43	- Error paths for external failures (network down, disk full, lock contention)
44	
45	## Force-unwrap only with compile-time-provable safety
46	
47	Never force-unwrap (`!`) on runtime values. For URL literals that are known-valid at compile time, use a clearly documented pattern:
48	
49	```swift
50	// Acceptable — string literal, provably valid
51	private static let apiBase = URL(string: "https://api.example.com")! // known-valid literal
52	
53	// BAD — runtime input, may fail
54	let url = URL(string: userInput)! // crash on bad input
55	```
56	
57	Even for literals, prefer a static `let` over inline force-unwrap to centralize the assumption.
58	
59	## Comment WHY, not WHAT
60	
61	Do not write comments that restate what the code does. Comments must explain *why* a non-obvious decision was made. If the code is self-explanatory, no comment is needed.
62	
63	```swift
64	// BAD
65	// Create the directory
66	try FileManager.default.createDirectory(at: dir, withIntermediateDirectories: true)
67	
68	// GOOD — no comment needed, the code is self-explanatory
69	
70	// GOOD — explains a non-obvious choice
71	// Using LOCK_NB to avoid blocking the cooperative thread pool; retry with backoff instead.
72	while flock(fd, LOCK_EX | LOCK_NB) != 0 { ... }
73	```
74	
75	## Name magic numbers
76	
77	Every numeric literal (other than 0, 1, true, false) must be a named constant explaining its purpose.
78	
79	```swift
80	// BAD
81	if fileSize > 5_242_880 { rotate() }
82	
83	// GOOD
84	private static let maxLogFileSizeBytes = 5_242_880 // 5 MB
85	if fileSize > Self.maxLogFileSizeBytes { rotate() }
86	```

3	public actor ClaudeStatusConnector: StatusConnector {
4	    nonisolated public let vendor: Vendor = .claude
5	
6	    private let logger: FileLogger
7	    private let session: URLSession
8	    private let endpointURLString: String
9	
10	    private static let requestTimeoutSeconds: TimeInterval = 5
11	    public static let defaultEndpoint = "https://status.anthropic.com/api/v2/incidents/unresolved.json"
12	
13	    public init(
14	        logger: FileLogger = Loggers.claude,
15	        session: URLSession = .shared,
16	        endpointURLString: String = ClaudeStatusConnector.defaultEndpoint
17	    ) {
18	        self.logger = logger
19	        self.session = session
20	        self.endpointURLString = endpointURLString
21	    }
22	
23	    public func fetchOutages() async throws -> [Outage] {
24	        guard let url = URL(string: endpointURLString) else {
25	            throw StatusConnectorError.invalidURL(rawValue: endpointURLString)
26	        }
27	        var request = URLRequest(url: url)
28	        request.timeoutInterval = Self.requestTimeoutSeconds
29	        request.setValue("application/json", forHTTPHeaderField: "Accept")
30	
31	        logger.log(.debug, "Fetching Claude status from \(endpointURLString)")
32	
33	        let data: Data
34	        let response: URLResponse
35	        do {
36	            (data, response) = try await session.data(for: request)
37	        } catch {
38	            logger.log(.warning, "Status endpoint network error: \(error)")
39	            throw StatusConnectorError.networkError(underlying: error, url: endpointURLString)
40	        }
41	
42	        let httpCode = (response as? HTTPURLResponse)?.statusCode ?? -1
43	        guard httpCode == 200 else {
44	            logger.log(.warning, "Status endpoint returned HTTP \(httpCode)")
45	            throw StatusConnectorError.unexpectedResponse(statusCode: httpCode, url: endpointURLString)
46	        }
47	
48	        let decoded: StatuspageIncidentsResponse
49	        do {
50	            decoded = try JSONDecoder().decode(StatuspageIncidentsResponse.self, from: data)
51	        } catch {
52	            logger.log(.error, "Status endpoint parse error: \(error)")
53	            throw StatusConnectorError.parseError(underlying: error, url: endpointURLString)
54	        }
55	
56	        let outages: [Outage] = decoded.incidents.compactMap { incident in
57	            // "none" impact means the incident is informational with no user-facing effect —
58	            // don't surface it as an outage in the UI.
59	            guard incident.impact != "none" else { return nil }
60	            let severity = OutageSeverity(rawValue: incident.impact)
61	            // Invalid shortlink must not drop the incident itself — surface it without href.
62	            let href: URL?
63	            if let raw = incident.shortlink {
64	                href = URL(string: raw)
65	            } else {
66	                href = nil
67	            }
68	            return Outage(
69	                vendor: vendor,
70	                errorMessage: incident.name,
71	                severity: severity,
72	                since: ISODate(rawValue: incident.created_at),
73	                href: href
74	            )
75	        }
76	        logger.log(.info, "Claude status: \(outages.count) active outage(s)")
77	        return outages
78	    }

I'm noticing those SourceKit diagnostics are outdated — the types are actually defined in the same module, so I'll verify everything works once I run a full build. For the UpdateInstaller, I need to handle two different paths: Homebrew requires launching a Terminal to run the upgrade command since the app can't replace itself while it's running, and I'm working through how to orchestrate that with osascript.

The cleanest approach is to spawn a shell script that waits a moment, runs the brew upgrade, and relaunches the app, then immediately quit the current instance. Since standard Homebrew cask installations are user-writable, I don't need to worry about sudo prompts. For the manual update path, I'll download the zip, verify its sha256 hash, unzip to a temporary location, use replaceItemAt to swap out the running bundle, and relaunch via the open command.

To keep this testable and respect the principle that every public method needs tests, I'll abstract the shell command generation into a separate method that AppDelegate can call, then use NSWorkspace or a detached Process to execute it without blocking. The key is spawning the subprocess with disowning so it survives after the app terminates, then calling NSApp.terminate immediately.

content.content:
1	import Foundation
2	
3	/// Drives `SnapshotRecorder` on a fixed cadence, independent of the user's
4	/// polling interval. Reads the current file state from `UsagesFileManager` and
5	/// forwards it to the recorder every tick.
6	public actor SnapshotScheduler {
7	    /// The roadmap pins the snapshot cadence at once per minute, decoupled from
8	    /// the user-configurable `RefreshInterval`. Kept internal so tests can override
9	    /// via the initializer.
10	    public static let defaultInterval: Duration = .seconds(60)
11	
12	    private let fileManager: UsagesFileManager
13	    private let recorder: any SnapshotRecording
14	    private let clock: ClockProvider
15	    private let interval: Duration
16	    private let logger: FileLogger
17	
18	    private var task: Task<Void, Never>?
19	
20	    public init(
21	        fileManager: UsagesFileManager,
22	        recorder: any SnapshotRecording,
23	        clock: ClockProvider = SystemClock(),
24	        interval: Duration = SnapshotScheduler.defaultInterval,
25	        logger: FileLogger = Loggers.app
26	    ) {
27	        self.fileManager = fileManager
28	        self.recorder = recorder
29	        self.clock = clock
30	        self.interval = interval
31	        self.logger = logger
32	    }
33	
34	    public func start() {
35	        guard task == nil else {
36	            logger.log(.debug, "SnapshotScheduler: already running")
37	            return
38	        }
39	        let interval = self.interval
40	        task = Task { [self] in
41	            while !Task.isCancelled {
42	                do {
43	                    try await Task.sleep(for: interval)
44	                } catch {
45	                    break
46	                }
47	                guard !Task.isCancelled else { break }
48	                await self.tickOnce()
49	            }
50	        }
51	        logger.log(.info, "SnapshotScheduler: started")
52	    }
53	
54	    public func stop() {
55	        task?.cancel()
56	        task = nil
57	    }
58	
59	    /// Single tick — public so callers can force a snapshot and tests can avoid
60	    /// waiting on the real timer.
61	    public func tickOnce() async {
62	        let file = await fileManager.read()
63	        await recorder.recordSnapshot(from: file, now: clock.now())
64	    }
65	}
